title: IFERROR
summary: IFERROR catches errors from a formula and returns a value you choose instead, keeping reports clean and professional. It traps errors like #N/A, #DIV/0!, and #VALUE!.
jobUseCases: Showing 'Not Found' instead of #N/A when a lookup fails on a dashboard
Displaying 0 or a blank when dividing by an empty cell to avoid #DIV/0!
Cleaning up imported data where some calculations produce errors
Presenting a friendly message in management reports instead of raw error codes
Preventing error cells from breaking downstream SUM or AVERAGE totals

examples.title: Friendly lookup failure
examples.explanation: If the VLOOKUP cannot find the value in G2, the cell shows 'Not Found' instead of #N/A. The first argument is the formula to try; the second is the fallback.

examples.title: Avoiding divide-by-zero
examples.explanation: If C2 is blank or zero, the division would error, so IFERROR returns 0 instead of #DIV/0!. This keeps the column clean for further calculations.

examples.title: Blank instead of error
examples.explanation: Returns an empty string when the multiplication errors (for example if a cell contains text), leaving the cell visually blank in the report.

examples.title: Wrapping INDEX + MATCH
examples.explanation: Catches the #N/A that MATCH throws when H2 is not found and shows 'No match' instead, so the dashboard stays tidy.

commonMistakes: Using IFERROR to hide genuine errors that signal a real data problem, masking bugs
Wrapping too broadly so a typo elsewhere in the formula is silently swallowed
Confusing IFERROR with IFNA; IFERROR catches all errors while IFNA catches only #N/A
Returning 0 when blank would be more appropriate, which skews averages and counts
Forgetting that older Excel versions used ISERROR with IF instead of IFERROR
practiceTips: Use IFNA when you only want to trap not-found errors and still see other problems
Keep the fallback value meaningful: 'Not Found' for lookups, 0 or blank for math
Prefer building the formula correctly first, then add IFERROR only for expected failures
